When you look at the portfolio, Russ, what name stand out to you as sort of representing the philosophy that you?
You espouse.

What are our largest weightings in the portfolio?
And some of those at the moment today would be oldings like Rainier, which is a Timberland company.
And it's about four million acres trading in a deep discount to what, you know, each one of these acres is actually worth.
Um, this is an industry that we've done well in for decades.
We've owned this company before in some of its predecessor companies.
Um, you know, the people side of things, they just came together through an actually intelligent merger with, uh, with potlatch Delta, which is another company that we, we owned.
That's how they came together to create our, our largest position.
Um, and then when it gets to price, you know, that's the business, that's the people side of things.
Um, we see something that's worth 30 to 40 trading at 20.
And it's, uh, you know, you could do various ways on per acre mathematics, but, um, it's, it's trading.
You know, well below, uh, private market, um, timberland values.
And it's a good store of value, by the way, that can do something for you.
Beyond other kind of, quote, stores of value that, you know, rest more on, um, people believing in them that people using them.
What's the reason for the discount?
Well, you know, this, this is somewhat of a theme.
And some of our other holdings as well.
It might not be the best public company.
Um, you know, the, the timberland industry, timber reats were created over 20 or so years ago.
It's a tax advantaged way to dividend out earnings from owning timberland, from cutting trees.
And when there were a few timberland companies, I think it was a little easier for the market to kind of wrap their heads all around them.
Now it's just down to warehouse, which is a big S&P 500 company that doesn't trade it as much of the discount.
Um, and then us right in here, where, um, people just don't know what to do with this company.
You, you know, go to, uh, reat week conferences and these guys are probably stuck in the back room somewhere.
And, uh, they're, they're not able to, you know, pouring them by reat standards.

Nobody likes reats anyway today.

But these space data centers, that'll, that'll be a whole new reat class that, uh, maybe what will invest a 10 years from now who does.
But, uh, but, but no, it's just, uh, it's, it's a great asset class because, um, when it's time to cut the trees you should, if it's not then you don't, but they keep growing in value.
That was kind of, since I'm coming back to the Jeremy Grantham thing, I guess, I don't know why.
But, but, you know, that was kind of consensus thinking with the Davison foundations, you know, 10, 20 years ago.

That's, that's not now, but that's the opportunity going for it. This is an understandable, you know, many decades, many generations type asset, um, and we get a chance to buy it cheaply in the public markets.
And who knows how that gets solved, you know, the company is buying back its own shares currently. That's one way to kind of slowly and steadily a creep value for share and close that gap.
They can do something bigger to close that gap. Could should this be owned privately at some point? We'll see.
One of the return on assets look like over, I don't know, called a decade kind of smooth out some of the timber prices.
You know, it's, it's never, it's, it's an interesting one because it can be both a good business and a low RWA type business where you've got that kind of steady baseline, um, to the value.
But, you know, because land is such a steady kind of asset claim, you see that in some reeds that are out there and it's like a land read trades of a, you know, real low dividend yield.
That's essentially what we've got within this company. So it's going to be a single digit, you know, RWA mid to high single digit RWA. Now at the price that we're paying, it can be better than that.
But, but over the long run, this is not, you know, some wonderful, you know, royalty patented type business or something like that.
How do you think about portfolio construction? How concentrated do you like to get at inception? How diversified do you like to be?
How do you think about diversification across industries and so on?
So, um, we, I, I think back to, to your book, concentrated investing. You know, there's this great. I'm, I'm one of the few readers.

Yeah. We found them. We found the guy who bought one. It's, it's a really good book. And I remember that I'm kind of coming for both angles here. So, so starting, um, starting at the most concentrated, you know, we've all in CFA class, seeing those studies where, you know,

10 to 15 carefully selected things after that, the incremental benefit from diversification starts dropping off. Now in real life, it's hard to find 10 to 15 things that aren't at least somewhat correlated. You know, but, but you start there.

Then kind of coming from, from the top, from the extra diversified world, you know, I think of that table in the book where once you start getting over 30 to 40 stocks, you mathematically look too much like the index.
And when you look too much like the index, you don't deserve to exist.
And it also gets hard to, we're a small team of generalists. We're always going to have five to 10 people on the research team. You know, we don't have a whole lot of different products or anything.
We've never been about, you know, product proliferation and asset gathering.

We've actually closed, had each one of our strategies closed at some point, you know, so, so we're, we're truly trying to be aligned with, with our partners. And as part of that, you concentrate on your best ideas, what you know best.
So that's somewhere between 10 and 35. We, we are around 20.
We found that is a good solid number to get appropriate diversification benefits to know what we own and to keep that high active share, which justifies being an active manager.
How big do you like to get an inception?
You know, it'll depend a little bit. So, you know, 20 or so stocks needs portfolio. If each one of them were equally weighted, that'd be about a 5% position or so.
If it's something where we are newer to the name, we'll start off smaller than that 5%.

One of the, the changes that we think will make our, you know, next 20, 30 years, better than we put in a few years ago, were some some upside position limits because kind of after we started going above, you know, 8% or so positions, we weren't adding enough value for the extra risk that was taken on by that concentration.
There's not going to do that anymore. But, you know, something smaller than 2 to 3%, not worth your time in a 20 stock portfolio.
Over 8%, we found that's a level where we weren't getting enough bang for our buck. So that's what it will be the range between those two.
And then if you're just staying in society, what are you sort of, where are you trimming on the other side?
You know, this, this is a helpful time to kind of bring in our price to value concept. You know, so each one of our individual investments, we appraise it.
And if we think it's worth 100 and it's trading at 60, it's a 60% price to value ratio.
Now each price to value ratio is not created equal. Leverage is a big factor on both of those. We also have the price to enterprise value.

We're an unleveraged $60.00 is better than a leveraged $60.00 because that leveraged $60.00 could be a $75.80 price to enterprise value, which would therefore be more risky and less attractive for future returns.
So, you know, generally we're going to be looking to sell those investments that are higher, P2V and lower quality.
You know, and quality can, can be a big driver of future value per share growth in free cash flow per share growth, which is a very important part of returns.
It can be one of those harder to quantify parts that doesn't show up in today's spreadsheet.

But up and down the portfolio today, we've got companies that are on offense that are begging for there to finally be some volatility that they can use to, you know, outpace their peers and grow their value per share and free cash flow per share.
You know, our portfolios on average over we've tracked this back decades have had a price to value ratio high 60s percent, which if we're looking for 60% or so, that kind of makes sense.
Today we're below 60%. That's unique. It's extra unique at a time when the S&P 500 is hitting highs.
So, we can have that kind of rare time again, why Joe had me on today, because we have good perspective, absolute and relative from here in our opinion.
Sorry, JT, I stepped on your toes before.
Oh, I don't know. I think I was going to talk about power laws a little bit when it's so like trimming things that are working to theoretically buy something that wasn't working.
So, just maybe more in general thoughts on reversion to the mean versus power laws.
Yeah. So, I mentioned a tough 12 months at the start. It's really kind of been a tough 12 years for the concept of reversion to the mean.

Yeah. We do still believe in that concept. I believe that we have learned, though, the virtue of, you know, holding on to something that you understand, that you trust the people that is on offense enough to keep growing its value per share.

That would be a good thing to hold sometimes at 80 to 90 cents. It doesn't have to be a big huge weight necessarily, but don't get too distracted, because we're value hounds, you know, by that new 60, 50, $0.50 that, you know, might not be on offense as much.
Yeah, and you probably don't know it as well to actually like pin down that number of and that is like there is a big big difference between having lived with something for a long time and not.
And it's just it's just one of those things it's hard to describe, but, but you know it once you've done it for a while.
And that that's also when you can make a whole lot of money to when you know something well and it just drops for silly reasons and then you can can take that waiting back up. That's good.

But the grass is always cheaper. Yeah, the saying is.

You know, have been becoming a little less permanent and that's interesting and that's, I would say encouraging for our style of investment, you know, interest rates have also been a factor here where.

It was just, it was kind of crazy, you know, but.
I still feel like some of that is residual in the environment right now with like.
People talking about 20 times being cheap for something it's like well.
That's that's relatively cheap, but I remember a long time ago and 20 was kind of considered expensive.

Great and understandable and then it goes to 15 to 20 times and that's great, but.
Like you said, it's just become so consensus that things are 25 30 and now they've come down to 20 folks that 20 can go to 10 or 12 so that's.
You take on a lot of risks now happens so.
I read an interesting stat here recently I didn't actually go dig it out to make sure it was true or not, but.
It was that 12% of the earnings of the S and P 500.
I don't remember it must be this most recent turn on 12 months were from three companies writing up their investments in things like anthropic and whatever all these other, you know,

that have come good do so how much that that would scare me right now a little bit if I was like underwriting the returns from here on, on an index fund when you've got a big chart of that is just purely a mark a market on something that's not that clear.
Well, I think that's a very good point like how you can get to a time in the market when you can lose serious money is when.

I think it fluctuates a little more briefly.

And what is the real kind of pricing per unit of a lot of this AI compute that is going on?
That's a really hard question.
That's the question.

And you know, that's that's one that we we will be interested in trying to to answer in the future.
But at these prices, we we we feel like it's.
You have to be right about it.
You know, you've got to have a really strong and firm opinion at these prices.
And sometimes you don't have to have to play.
You can own a grocery store at seven times free cash flow or a food brand at 10 times free cash flow.
We'd rather do that.
What does what is quality mean to you guys?
How do you think about quality?
I think about it a whole lot.
You know, that's why it goes business people price in that order.
And you know, we want that that quality coming from both business and people.
Sometimes it'll come more from one than the other.
But again, we prefer both, you know, on the business side.
It starts with that understandability and ability to grow.
You know, can you be confident that this will be something that can generate more true cash earnings power five to 10 years from now?
And how will it get there?
What will the competitive environment look like?
Do their customers actually like them?
Or kind of begrudgingly do business with them?
Which they prefer.
Well, that's, I mean, you're joking, but there is something about a monopoly where they do have to do business with you that might be.
Might be just as attractive, right?
It could be, although you want that mutual benefit flow in both ways.
Yeah, the less they like you, the more that they'll be thinking about ways to ultimately stop doing business with you.
Like you're throwing, yeah.
Yeah.
So, so it's things like that.
And then some of those things are quantifiable and some of them are not, you know, you can quantify pricing power to agree.
But, you know, sometimes you kind of just got to spit ball a little bit.
You can quantify return on capital in a backwards looking way, but what matters is forward looking return on incremental capital.
And then that gets the people side of things where.
We want owners alongside of us who are going to unconventionally grow the value per share and grow the free cash flow per share.
I mean, there's a lot of crazy stock market stuff out there.
We've already been riffing on, but we haven't seen growing your free cash flow per share over the long run stop working.
And if you do that, you'll generally get rewarded.
You can have quarter or a year when that gets gets out of whack versus the stock price.
If you can do that, you'll be in good shape.
And so, you know, we can have a company like CNX resources that natural gas production and owning pipelines is not necessarily the world's greatest business.
But we are highly confident that this board and this management team gets it on value per share creation.
And we'll do intelligent things and not put the company at risk.
And that's why it's been, you know, really good holding for us.
And that's also though how it can still be undervalued because they'll be willing to hedge.
You know, a lot of other oil and gas companies will say, oh, our investors want exposure to the commodity or something.
Well, we it's not these to want exposure to growing free cash flow per share and low risk.
And if you can do that with hedging, you should do it.

You know, on the other side of things, you know, when we've got a truly, truly great business, you might not need the world's best manager necessarily, although we don't want to make any excuses though on that side of things.
You know, we've had some some great, we've had some average, we prefer great on both.
Ross, you think in a world where there's perhaps faster disruption, more change, more less competitively advanced periods to underwrite, like maybe they're shorter.
Maybe does management matter more than if you can't depend on the business as much as maybe you could have 20, 30 years ago.
I think that's probably true.
Just don't figure it out like you just find someone smart and they'll figure out how to make it work.
That's right. And it's also management also matters more the more you were required to recycle that capital, you know, right.
You know, these capital recycling businesses like banks, insurance, natural resources, you have to get the people right in those because if you don't, you're in trouble.
Yeah, especially the ones where they take the money today and then they give a promise of giving it back later.
You really have to have that right.

Yeah, that's right. There's a big difference between an insurance company run by our peat and good friends at White Mountains and a bunch of other random ones and a bank run by Jamie Dimon or a bank run by who knows who.
Yeah.

Just stay there for a year.
All right, so first I need to do a shout out to my dear friend Dan who did probably 90% of the work on this segment.
And so if this ends up being a banger all the credit goes to him.
So we opened the scene in Manchester, England, it's 1883.
And this bearded engineer professor guy named Osborne Reynolds is standing over a long glass pipe.
And in that pipe runs out of a big tank of water that's like perfectly still water.
And he's rigged up this tiny nozzle that injects a thread of dyed water right into the center that is flowing inside of this pipe.
So and he opens his valve and the water starts moving down the pipe through it.
And basically like you can kind of see this little line of dye that is running down the middle.
And now before I go any further I'll admit up front that you know we're about to torture the concept of fluid dynamics with analogies.
So you know I can already hear the three engineers we have listening that are unsubscribing already.
But that's fair and stick with me because I think this one actually holds in this kind of interesting.
So back to Reynolds, he opens the valve of crack and that there's this slow flow.
And the dye you know is quite beautiful actually like it's just this perfect running the whole length of the pipe.
A single clean straight line picture of being maybe red inside of the water.
And the water is moving around it in these very orderly parallel sheets.
Each layer is sliding past the next nothing's really mixing.
And engineers call this laminar flow.
L-A-M-I-N-A-R.
And it's smooth, predictable, boring kind of in the best way.
And then Reynolds opens the valve a little wider and the water is now flowing a little faster through here.
And then there's a specific speed that it starts going that the thread starts to wobble a little bit.
And you've probably seen this before with like a candle you know that is you know there's no real air disturbing.
And it's kind of like a perfect little clean smoke that's coming out.
And then like as you get more air starts wobbling it's the same concept.
You know it starts shut so we're back to the water tank and it starts shuddering.
And then it like bursts apart into this massive like swirling eddies that fill the entire pipe.
And that's turbulence chaos and that clean line is completely gone.
And here's so here's why you know we're still using Reynolds's name 140 years later.
He figured out that the switch from smooth to chaotic doesn't just depend on the speed alone.
It also depends on a specific ratio that's now called the Reynolds number.
And at the top of that equation in the numerator you've got the inertial forces.
And inertia you know it's kind of can maybe trip you up in this instance because it isn't the fluid like wanting to go necessarily in a straight line like you think of inertia.
It's that when it starts wobbling it starts to have a sideways momentum and it starts.
These little packets of water imagine them kind of bumping into each other and nudging each other out of the way.
And they run into their neighbors and they knock them off course and now this wobble grows into that swirling eddy.
